frontmatter.title: Part 3: The Poisson Distribution — The Limit of Many Rare Events

children.value: An Applied Scenario — Bearing Faults Per Hour

children.label: An Applied Scenario — Bearing Faults Per Hour

children.value: A different sensor on the same motor monitors
— the high-frequency clicks a damaged ball bearing emits as it rolls past a load zone. On a healthy bearing, these clicks are extremely rare. On a damaged one, they multiply.
how many shock pulses arrive in a one-hour window?
Try modelling this with the Binomial. You’d need:

children.html: nnn

children.value: — the number of “trials” per hour. But what is a trial? A nanosecond? A bearing rotation? It depends on how finely you slice time.

children.html: ppp

children.value: — the probability of a click in one trial. As you slice time finer,

children.html: nnn
ppp
λ=3\lambda = 3λ=3

children.value: clicks/hour on a healthy bearing — is a real, measurable physical quantity. It doesn’t depend on how you slice time.
You want a distribution parameterised by

children.html: λ\lambdaλ

children.value: alone, where the trial-counting machinery quietly disappears. That distribution exists, and it’s what the Binomial becomes in exactly this regime.
The Binomial works perfectly when you know

children.html: nnn
ppp

children.value: separately. But many counting problems involve events that are
— and the region’s “size” is what you actually measure, not

ppp

children.value: . This single fact is the engine behind every shot-noise calculation in sensors, every queueing model in networks, and every count-based regression in statistics.

children.html: λ=3\lambda = 3λ=3
3≈1.73\sqrt{3} \approx 1.733​≈1.73
P(0 clicks)=e−3≈0.050P(0 \text{ clicks}) = e^{-3} \approx 0.050P(0 clicks)=e−3≈0.050
P(≥10 clicks)≈0.0011P(\geq 10 \text{ clicks}) \approx 0.0011P(≥10 clicks)≈0.0011

children.value: So observing 0 clicks in an hour is uncommon but not alarming (5% of healthy hours look like this). Observing 10 clicks in an hour is a 1-in-1000 event under the healthy model — strong evidence the bearing state has changed.

children.value: , runs every condition-monitoring threshold in the building.
Why Poisson is the Right Model for Photon Counting

children.label: Why Poisson is the Right Model for Photon Counting

children.value: photons per second. The probability that any specific photon reaches a 6 µm × 6 µm photosite is vanishingly small. But the product

children.html: λ=np\lambda = npλ=np

children.value: — determined by illumination, reflectance, exposure time, and sensor area — sits in the range of tens to thousands.
This is exactly the Poisson regime: enormous

children.html: nnn
ppp
λ\lambdaλ

children.value: . The Poisson model is not an approximation here — it is the physically correct distribution for photon counting.
The Poisson Has ONE Parameter — Why That Matters

children.label: The Poisson Has ONE Parameter — Why That Matters

children.value: If you measure the mean of a Poisson process, you immediately know its variance. Sensor designers use this constantly — they predict noise from signal level alone. The same trick works for any system in the Poisson regime: a packet-loss monitor, a click-rate model, or a defect-counting pipeline.
